anvaya.meaning: of one whose self is not conquered

translations.verbatim_quote: For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 941 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The purpose of practicing eightfold yoga is to control the mind in order to make it a friend in discharging the human mission. Unless the mind is controlled, the practice of yoga (for show) is simply a waste of time.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.6 Tasya, of him; yena, by whom; jitah, has been conquered, subdued; his eva atma, very self, the aggregate of body and organs; that atma, self; is bandhuh, the friend; atmanah, of his self. The idea is that he is a conqueror of his senses.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.6 For him by whom the very self has been conquered by the very self, of him the self is the friend; but for one whose self is unconquered, the self itself remains in a state of enmity, like an external foe.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.6 A person whose mind is conquered by himself in relation to sense-objects, has that mind as his friend. In the case of one whose mind is not conquered in this way, his own mind, like an enemy, remains hostile. The meaning is that it acts, against his attainment of supreme beatitude.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.6 The mind that has been subdued by the higher discriminating self is the friend of that very self; but the mind that has not been subdued behaves as the enemy of the self, opposing every attempt at concentration.

positions.rendering: the unconquered self remains in a state of enmity, like an external foe — actively obstructing
the unsubdued mind behaves as the enemy of the self, opposing every attempt at concentration
the unconquered mind remains the greatest enemy

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three preserve the bidirectional claim — friend AND enemy. The crucial nuance preserved: the unconquered self is not just unhelpful, it acts in active enmity (śatru-vat). Sectarian divergence is on whether 'self' refers to the empirical self (Shankara, Ramanuja) or specifically the mind (Prabhupada).

literal_meaning: For one by whom the self has been conquered by the self, the self is the friend of the self. But for one whose self is unconquered, the self itself acts as enemy, hostile-like.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the bidirectional doctrine. The conquered self is the friend; the unconquered self is the enemy. The verse is sharp on both sides — the unconquered self is not just unhelpful, it acts in active enmity (śatru-vat, like an enemy). This is the verse the engineering analog must preserve in both directions: years of disciplined practice produce a self-system that supports the work; years of fragmentation produce a self-system that obstructs the work.
